posts.name: Dave McCloskey
posts.cooked:
I’ve sampled approximately 30-40 2013 Cabs and red blends from the Napa & Sonoma area over the past 12 months. Prior to the release of the 2013 vintage I read multiple wine maker accounts of this vintage, most believed it could surpass 2012 for overall quality. I also read several accounts that suggested the weather conditions were remarkably similar to 2012. This puzzled me, because generally speaking I’m finding the 2013 Nor Cal vintage to be more austere with increased levels of acidity, tannins and lower forwardness of fruit. In general more age worthy wines that are not drinking particularly well in their youth or at least not showing their full potential. Someone recently told me the weather was cooler in 2013, which would account for the general changes in profile.

Is this the case of a difference in weather or was the 2013 vintage the start of a transition away from typical California fruit forward wines and a more conscience movement towards an old world style?

posts.name: Eric LaMasters
posts.cooked: Your assessment of 2013 being higher in acid, tannins, and less fruit-forward is the same as my impression and, from what I have read from others, is pretty much the general consensus. It was definitely a product of the vintage conditions rather than a change in style. The 2014s are a lot more forward than the 2013s. The 2013s will be great, but they will need a lot of time to achieve their potential.

posts.name: mark rudner
posts.cooked:
side by side schrader '12’s and 13’s confirms your thoughts on the 13’s.
the 12 was singing while the 13 will seemingly take years to come around.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Thicker skins, (concomitant) unusually high levels of tannin, lower yields.

posts.name: Brian Tuite  GOML!
posts.cooked: My impressions have been '13 is a more structured vintage for Napa cab.

user_status.description: Wine doesn’t have to be tertiary to be enjoyable

posts.name: Cameron Hughes
posts.cooked:
Dave McCloskey:

I’ve sampled approximately 30-40 2013 Cabs and red blends from the Napa & Sonoma area over the past 12 months. Prior to the release of the 2013 vintage I read multiple wine maker accounts of this vintage, most believed it could surpass 2012 for overall quality. I also read several accounts that suggested the weather conditions were remarkably similar to 2012. This puzzled me, because generally speaking I’m finding the 2013 Nor Cal vintage to be more austere with increased levels of acidity, tannins and lower forwardness of fruit. In general more age worthy wines that are not drinking particularly well in their youth or at least not showing their full potential. Someone recently told me the weather was cooler in 2013, which would account for the general changes in profile.

Is this the case of a difference in weather or was the 2013 vintage the start of a transition away from typical California fruit forward wines and a more conscience movement towards an old world style?

Quality is, of course, subjective but I would parse the vintages this way…'12 and '13 were powerful vintages with big alc, acids and tannins but '13 was more powerful/concentrated on all fronts. It will be interesting to see how the '13’s age…they have extremely high alcs (funny thing, BRIX were on par with '12 but conversion was especially high so alc’s good half point higher or more across the board vs our '12 wines).

I just had a customer return my favorite cab my team made out of '13 (Lot 598 Meteor Vineyard) complaining it didn’t live up to his expectations. I went back through and tasted all our '13 SVD’s and sure enough, they are massive walls of tannin right now and very much shut down. If you don’t know how to taste these wines I suspect most folks would find them disappointing. I suspect most of the Napa '13 Cabernets would be shut down and rather impenetrable right now.

'14, on the other hand, is an open knit vintage with moderate yet ample tannins and sexy, vibrant fruit. Take it with a grain of salt but, I love 'em.

posts.name: Drew Goin
posts.cooked: I say drink the 2012’s/14’s, and age your 2013’s.

posts.name: @lbert Reg0
posts.cooked: What he ^^^ said. I’m drinking 2010, 2012 and 2014’s before my 2013’s. Let’s hope the 13’s live up to the hype down the road.

posts.name: Justin Wittkopf
posts.cooked: I guess it all depends on what you like in a cab. Everything that has been said above about the 2013 vintage sounds much more appealing to me and what I like than that the 2012 vintage. I like my cabs with plenty of structure and acid, but I also like to age my cabs for several years before opening them. When I hear of a 2012 being ready to go now my heart sinks a bit. I’ve found these wines that are ready to go early often become jammy messes after a few years. YMMV.

posts.name: Merrill Lindquist
posts.cooked:
Eric LaMasters:

Your assessment of 2013 being higher in acid, tannins, and less fruit-forward is the same as my impression and, from what I have read from others, is pretty much the general consensus. It was definitely a product of the vintage conditions rather than a change in style. The 2014s are a lot more forward than the 2013s. The 2013s will be great, but they will need a lot of time to achieve their potential.

2013s are not at their best right now (based on my Black Cats). 2014s are really fun and approachable…but for how long? They are both mere babies.

posts.name: Dave McCloskey
posts.cooked:
Cameron Hughes:
Dave McCloskey:

I’ve sampled approximately 30-40 2013 Cabs and red blends from the Napa & Sonoma area over the past 12 months. Prior to the release of the 2013 vintage I read multiple wine maker accounts of this vintage, most believed it could surpass 2012 for overall quality. I also read several accounts that suggested the weather conditions were remarkably similar to 2012. This puzzled me, because generally speaking I’m finding the 2013 Nor Cal vintage to be more austere with increased levels of acidity, tannins and lower forwardness of fruit. In general more age worthy wines that are not drinking particularly well in their youth or at least not showing their full potential. Someone recently told me the weather was cooler in 2013, which would account for the general changes in profile.

Is this the case of a difference in weather or was the 2013 vintage the start of a transition away from typical California fruit forward wines and a more conscience movement towards an old world style?

Quality is, of course, subjective but I would parse the vintages this way…'12 and '13 were powerful vintages with big alc, acids and tannins but '13 was more powerful/concentrated on all fronts. It will be interesting to see how the '13’s age…they have extremely high alcs (funny thing, BRIX were on par with '12 but conversion was especially high so alc’s good half point higher or more across the board vs our '12 wines).

I just had a customer return my favorite cab my team made out of '13 (Lot 598 Meteor Vineyard) complaining it didn’t live up to his expectations. I went back through and tasted all our '13 SVD’s and sure enough, they are massive walls of tannin right now and very much shut down. If you don’t know how to taste these wines I suspect most folks would find them disappointing. I suspect most of the Napa '13 Cabernets would be shut down and rather impenetrable right now.

'14, on the other hand, is an open knit vintage with moderate yet ample tannins and sexy, vibrant fruit. Take it with a grain of salt but, I love 'em.

Great response, thank you for the insight!

I agree with Drew, I’m drinking my '12’s and '14’s and hanging on to the 11’s and 13’s. Watch out for the 2011’s from the really good producers. I suspect by 2021 and beyond they’ll be very intriguing indeed.

posts.name: Brian Armston
posts.cooked:
Agreed- the 2012’s are ready to go for the most part. I’m letting the '13s rest for a couple more years, and now I’m focusing on the '14s. I’ll be dropping some lists at the turn of the year and going deeper/more narrow.

The plan as of now is to take 2015 off (except for say Futo, Kata, Nine Suns, and a couple choice others who always bring good product to the table no matter the vintage), and trying to back fill with some choice 11’s. My thought (hope) is that by then, not only will some of them have rounded more into form (especially from higher elevations like say Dunn or Barnett), but the price on the secondary will be much lower since everyone will still be chasing the 13’s and 14’s.

That’s the plan, but of course, I will most likely deviate…
